subject: Chemistry
title: Nuclear Fusion: Powering the Stars
description: Learn Nuclear Fusion: Powering the Stars in one minute.

hook.text: The Sun fuses 600 million tons of hydrogen into helium every second. Fusion is the energy source of every star.

buildup.text: At 15 million degrees, hydrogen nuclei move fast enough to overcome electromagnetic repulsion and fuse together.

discovery.text: Four hydrogen nuclei fuse into one helium nucleus. The helium weighs less—the missing mass becomes pure energy via E = mc².

twist.text: Fusion releases 4 times more energy per gram than fission. But we still can't sustain it on Earth—containment is the problem.

climax.text: ITER in France is building a reactor that uses magnetic fields to contain 150-million-degree plasma. First tests aim for 2035.

punchline.text: Smash light atoms together, lose mass, gain energy. The Sun's recipe for power.

quiz.question: Why is nuclear fusion so difficult to achieve on Earth?
options: It requires extreme temperatures and we can't contain the plasma effectively
The fuel (hydrogen) is too rare on Earth
Fusion reactions produce too much radiation to be safe
